perturbation.original_text: 7. “Non-Disclosure Certificate” means the certificate annexed hereto by which Reviewing Representatives seeking access to Confidential Information shall certify their understanding that such access to Confidential Information is provided pursuant to the terms and conditions of this Agreement and that each Reviewing Representative has read this Agreement and agrees to be bound by it.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. “Non-Disclosure Certificate” means a document, which may or may not be annexed hereto, that Reviewing Representatives and any other individuals granted access to Confidential Information might be requested to sign. This certificate, if required, serves only as an acknowledgment of receipt of the Confidential Information and does not bind the signer to any specific terms or conditions beyond acknowledging receipt.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Non-Disclosure Certificate' makes it mandatory for Reviewing Representatives and binds them to the agreement's terms. The revised definition makes it optional, extends it to other individuals, and removes the binding aspect, turning it into a mere acknowledgment of receipt. This creates an in-text contradiction regarding the enforceability of confidentiality obligations, especially for non-Reviewing Representatives who access the information.
perturbation.contradicted_location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 4
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Discussions of Confidential Information. Parties and Reviewing Representatives who have executed this Agreement or a Non-Disclosure Certificate may discuss Confidential Information with other Parties and Reviewing Representatives who have executed this Agreement or a Non-Disclosure Certificate.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a 'Non-Disclosure Certificate' for Reviewing Representatives, binding them to the agreement's terms. The changed text makes this certificate optional and extends it to others without binding them, creating a contradiction. The contradicted text states that only parties who executed the agreement or a 'Non-Disclosure Certificate' may discuss confidential information. The changed text weakens the definition of 'Non-Disclosure Certificate' making it unclear whether signing the certificate still grants the right to discuss confidential information. This direct conflict over the certificate's function creates a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. “Reviewing Representative” means an employee of a Party who has signed or electronically signed a Non-Disclosure Certificate and who is a principal, partner, officer, director, employee, agent and other representative of a Party. Reviewing Representative may include a person whose duties include Competitive Duties so long as such Reviewing Representative’s receipt and use of Confidential Information is not prohibited conduct under FERC’s rules, including Standards of Conduct.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. “Reviewing Representative” means any individual, regardless of their relationship to a Party (including but not limited to consultants, contractors, and advisors), who is granted access to Confidential Information. Signing a Non-Disclosure Certificate is not required. Reviewing Representatives are explicitly permitted to use Confidential Information for competitive purposes, even if such use is prohibited under FERC’s rules or Standards of Conduct.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition requires a signed Non-Disclosure Certificate and compliance with FERC rules. The modified definition removes the signing requirement, broadens the scope to include individuals beyond employees, and permits competitive use of information, directly contradicting the original safeguards and creating uncertainty about the legal obligations of those accessing the information.
perturbation.contradicted_location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 2
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Reviewing Representative. A Reviewing Representative shall not have access to any Confidential Information unless that Reviewing Representative is required to have the information in order to carry out that person’s Transmission Functions responsibilities and has executed the attached Non-Disclosure Certificate. The Reviewing Representative shall deliver a copy of his or her executed Non-Disclosure Certificate to PJM. A Reviewing Representative shall not use the Confidential Information or any portion thereof to give any Party or a competitor of any Party a competitive or commercial advantage. A Reviewing Representative may make copies or Notes of Confidential Information that shall be subject to this Agreement. In the event a Reviewing Representative ceases to be employed or engaged by a Party, or is employed, retained, or given duties that include Competitive Duties, (i) the Reviewing Representative shall continue to comply with the terms and conditions of this Agreement with respect to the Confidential Information to which such person previously had access, (ii) the Party shall terminate the Reviewing Representative's access to Confidential Information, and (iii) the Party shall cause the Reviewing Representative to return or dispose of the Confidential Information, or transfer the information to another Reviewing Representative of the Party. Each Party shall advise the Reviewing Representatives that Confidential Information is confidential and shall be treated as confidential in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text. The original text requires a signed Non-Disclosure Certificate and compliance with FERC rules for 'Reviewing Representatives'. The changed definition removes the signing requirement, broadens the scope to include individuals beyond employees, and permits competitive use of information, even if prohibited under FERC. The contradicted text explicitly states that a 'Reviewing Representative' shall not have access to Confidential Information unless they have executed a Non-Disclosure Certificate, which directly conflicts with the change.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Non-Disclosure to Third Parties. Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to a third party without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party. Each Party shall treat all Confidential Information in every form as confidential, and shall not reveal, divulge or disclose Confidential Information, at any time or for any reason, to any third person or entity. This provision shall survive the expiration, termination or cancellation of this Agreement in accordance with Section 9.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Non-Disclosure to Third Parties. Parties are encouraged to share Confidential Information with third parties as deemed necessary for business development and strategic partnerships. The requirement for prior written approval from the Disclosing Party is waived. This provision does not survive the expiration, termination, or cancellation of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause strictly prohibits disclosure to third parties without prior written approval. The modified clause encourages disclosure, waives the approval requirement, and removes the survival clause. This creates a direct in-text contradiction regarding the obligation to maintain confidentiality with third parties, raising uncertainty about post-termination obligations.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 5
perturbation.contradicted_location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 6
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Defend Against Third Party Requests. Each Party and Reviewing Representative shall defend against disclosure of Confidential Information pursuant to any Third Party Request through all available legal processes, including, but not limited to, seeking to obtain any necessary protective orders. Each Party and Reviewing Representative shall provide PJM, and PJM shall provide each Disclosing Party, with prompt notice of any such Third Party Request or legal proceedings, and shall consult with PJM and/or any Disclosing Party in its efforts to deny the request or defend against such legal process. In the event a protective order or other remedy is denied, each Party agrees to furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information which its legal counsel advises PJM (and of which PJM shall, in turn, advise any Disclosing Party) in writing is legally required to be furnished, and to exercise their best efforts to obtain assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 5 encourages disclosure of confidential information to third parties and waives the prior written approval requirement, directly contradicting Section 6, which requires each party to defend against disclosure of Confidential Information pursuant to any Third Party Request. This creates a strong and clear contradiction because Section 5 now promotes sharing what Section 6 obligates the parties to protect. The changed text in Section 5 breaks compatibility with Section 6. The original text of Section 5 forbade sharing information, which is compatible with the contradicted text in Section 6.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Ownership and Use of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information delivered by a Disclosing Party to a Party pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party, and such Confidential Information shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party upon request. That portion of the Confidential Information that may be found in analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by or for a Party and all Confidential Information that is oral will be kept by a Party subject to the terms of this Agreement or destroyed. Neither the Party nor its Reviewing Representatives shall use the Confidential Information for any purpose whatsoever except for the purpose of executing the Reviewing Party’s Transmission Functions. Once the Party no longer requires the use of such information for the purpose of performing its Transmission Functions, the Confidential Information shall be returned or destroyed in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Ownership and Use of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information delivered by a Disclosing Party to a Party pursuant to this Agreement shall immediately become the property of the receiving Party, and the Disclosing Party relinquishes all rights to it. The receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy the Confidential Information at any time. The Party and its representatives may use the Confidential Information for any purpose, including purposes unrelated to Transmission Functions, and for their own financial gain.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the original ownership and use restrictions. It transfers ownership to the receiving party, removes the obligation to return or destroy the information, and allows unrestricted use, including for financial gain. This creates a significant in-text contradiction regarding the Disclosing Party's rights and the permissible uses of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 8
perturbation.contradicted_location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 9
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Identification of Confidential Information . Confidential Information that is in writing or other tangible form (including electronic form) shall be subject to this Agreement only if it is clearly marked as "Confidential" when disclosed by the Disclosing Party to a Party. Confidential Information that is provided orally shall be subject to this Agreement only if its confidential nature is announced at the time of disclosure and an outline of the scope of the information provided is reduced to writing, with a copy provided to the Party within ten (10) calendar days of oral disclosure of the information. Inadvertent failure to mark Confidential Information as “Confidential” at the time it is disclosed shall not be deemed a waiver by the Disclosing Party of the protections of this Agreement provided that such Confidential Information is identified and marked "Confidential" promptly upon the discovery of its inadvertent disclosure. Confidential Information excludes any information that: (i) the Disclosing Party has not specifically notified the Party is confidential; (ii) becomes available to the Party or the Reviewing Representative on a non-confidential basis from a source other than: (a) the Disclosing Party, or other person acting on behalf of the Disclosing Party; or (b) a Party who has confidentiality obligations to the Disclosing Party; (iii) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by the Party or its Reviewing Representatives; (iv) was previously known to the Party or its Reviewing Representative free and clear of any obligation to keep it confidential; (v) is disclosed to third parties by the Disclosing Party without restriction or obligation of confidentiality; (vi) is developed independently by the Party as evidenced by documentation made in the ordinary course of business by the Party; or (vii) the Disclosing Party notifies the Party that such information is no longer Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 8 directly contradicts Section 9. Section 8 now states that Confidential Information immediately becomes the property of the receiving party and they have no obligation to return or destroy it, with unrestricted use allowed. Section 9, however, specifies the conditions under which information is even considered "Confidential Information" subject to the agreement (e.g., marking as "Confidential"), implying that not all information passed is automatically confidential or subject to restrictions. The changed text in Section 8 eliminates the need for the conditions specified in Section 9, creating a direct conflict and thus, a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10 . Term of Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect unless and until terminated by the Parties. The obligations of the Parties under this Agreement shall continue and survive the Transmission Function s for which the Confidential Information was disclosed and shall remain binding under this Agreement unless disclosure is permitted under Section 8 or required by Law or Authority. Nothing herein shall be construed to limit the term of protection of Confidential Information otherwise protected by Law or Authority.
perturbation.changed_text: 10 . Term of Agreement. This Agreement shall automatically terminate 30 days after the effective date, regardless of whether the Transmission Functions have been completed or not. The obligations of the Parties under this Agreement cease entirely upon termination, with no surviving obligations. The Parties explicitly agree to waive any and all protections for Confidential Information after termination, regardless of whether such protections would otherwise be available under Law or Authority.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that the agreement remains in effect until terminated and that obligations survive the Transmission Functions. The modified clause sets a fixed termination date of 30 days, eliminates all surviving obligations, and waives all protections for Confidential Information after termination. This is a direct in-text contradiction regarding the duration of the agreement and the persistence of confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 10
perturbation.contradicted_location: B. Confidential Information - General Non-Disclosure Provisions, 5
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Non-Disclosure to Third Parties. Parties are encouraged to share Confidential Information with third parties as deemed necessary for business development and strategic partnerships. The requirement for prior written approval from the Disclosing Party is waived. This provision does not survive the expiration, termination, or cancellation of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original clause states the agreement remains until terminated and obligations survive Transmission Functions. The changed clause sets a termination date of 30 days and eliminates surviving obligations, which directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which states that 'This provision does not survive the expiration, termination, or cancellation of this Agreement.' The change creates a strong in-text contradiction regarding the agreement's duration and confidentiality obligations.
